title: Abraham Sees that Yahweh Sees: A Rambling Introduction
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander
source_files: Abraham Sees that Yahweh Sees - A Rambling Introduction - Elder Scott Hollander - Genesis 22_1-19.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Theme and Its Connection to John 8:56
subpoints: The study connects Genesis 22 with John 8:56: 'Your father Abraham rejoiced that he would see my day; he saw it and was glad'
Most commentators tie John 8:56 to one of the visions of the Abrahamic covenant but do not develop the Genesis 22 connection in detail
Fourth-to-ninth-century Syriac church fathers do make this connection in their writings
Purpose and application: gain a better understanding of Scripture, see God's faithfulness to his promises, understand that Yahweh who sees sees to it
We will see the connection between Christ as the ultimate sacrifice, the Abrahamic covenant, and the hope of resurrection

outline.title: Verse 1: God Tests Abraham
subpoints: The narrator tells us this is a test — announced to the reader but not to Abraham; this is outside God's normal interaction with people
Testing in Scripture often involves unique, challenging series of events — as with Job (testing from suffering) and Jesus (forty days in the wilderness)
The pattern of immediate obedience: God says 'go now' and Abraham went — active obedience before receiving all the details
The question of whether Abraham knew he would not have to kill Isaac or believed God would resurrect him (Hebrews 11:17-19 resolves this)

outline.title: Verse 2: Your Son, Your Only Son
subpoints: The redundancy 'your son, your only son Isaac whom you love' emphasizes the magnitude of the sacrifice and echoes New Testament language for Christ
Isaac is the 'only son' not because Abraham had no other children — Ishmael existed — but because he is the son of promise from Sarah
Genesis 21:12 and 15:4 specify the seed will come through Sarah, making Isaac uniquely the heir of promise; Ishmael had already been sent away
Moriah is a specific destination God will show Abraham — the journey involves faith in a destination not yet revealed, just as Abram left Ur not knowing where he was going

outline.title: Verses 3-8: The Journey and the Three Days
subpoints: The three-day journey: figuratively, Isaac was dead to Abraham from the moment God gave the command; Hebrews 11 interprets it this way
Abraham's statement to the young men: 'the lad and I will go yonder and worship, and we will come back to you' — he believed both would return
Hebrews 11:19 explains: Abraham concluded that God was able to raise Isaac from the dead, which is also why he could say 'we will come back'
Verse 8: 'God will provide for himself the lamb for a burnt offering, my son' — God is the subject; the emphasis is on God as actor, not on a substitute lamb yet in view
Abraham telling Isaac 'God will provide for himself the lamb' is likely honest faith rather than deliberate concealment; on the way down it becomes prophetic

outline.title: Verses 9-12: The Altar, the Knife, and 'Now I Know'
subpoints: The narrative slows in verse 9 — each step of building the altar and binding Isaac is captured, building tension to the climax of the raised knife
The phrase 'Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slay his son' may describe either reaching for the knife or placing his hand on Isaac's head (Leviticus 1:4)
Consideration of artwork depicting the scene: some show Abraham's hand on Isaac's head in a tender gesture — compelling given that Isaac is his beloved son
Hebrew wordplay: the word for 'angel' (*malach*) and 'knife' (*ma'achelet*) are phonetically similar — the angel of the LORD stopped the knife
The angel of the LORD is the pre-incarnate Christ — Yahweh himself calling out to stop the sacrifice

outline.title: Isaac as Type of Christ: The Parallels Introduced
subpoints: Isaac carried his own sacrificial wood upon his back just as Christ carried the cross
The fire and knife remained in the father's hands — even in Christ's willing sacrifice, the Father wielded judgment
Whatever Isaac's age — ranging from a teenager to thirty-seven based on the surrounding narrative — he was stronger than Abraham and went willingly
The implication of his willing submission is that he chose obedience to his father, mirroring Christ's willing sacrifice
Next session will cover: the full meaning of Yahweh Yireh, the significance of Mount Moriah, and the covenant expansion in verses 17-18

summary:
In this first session of a two-part study, Elder Scott Hollander introduces Genesis 22:1-19 through the lens of John 8:56, where Jesus declares that Abraham 'rejoiced that he would see my day; he saw it and was glad.' Most commentators connect this vision to the Abrahamic covenant appearances in Genesis 12-17, but Elder Hollander argues — drawing on Syriac church fathers and careful attention to the text — that Jesus was specifically pointing back to Genesis 22, where Abraham saw the day of Christ through the typological events of the binding of Isaac.

The session walks verse by verse through the passage, examining the significance of the test announcement in verse 1, the remarkable redundancy of 'your son, your only son Isaac whom you love' and why Isaac counts as Abraham's only son despite Ishmael's existence, and the three-day figurative death of Isaac from the moment God gave the command (interpreted explicitly by Hebrews 11:17-19 as Abraham expecting literal death and resurrection). The lesson also explores Abraham's statement to the young men — 'we will come back to you' — as a declaration of faith in resurrection, and verse 8's 'God will provide for himself the lamb' as emphasizing God as the actor while possibly being prophetic beyond Abraham's understanding at that moment.

The session slows over verses 9-12, noting the deliberate narrative deceleration and possible Hebrew wordplay between *malach* (angel) and *ma'achelet* (knife), and introduces the question of what it means for Abraham to have 'stretched out his hand' — whether reaching for the knife or placing his hand on Isaac's head in a gesture from Leviticus 1:4. The session closes by previewing the next lesson's topics: the full meaning of Yahweh Yireh, Mount Moriah's geographical connection to the crucifixion, and the covenant expansions of verses 17-18.
transcript:
## Introduction: Purpose and Application

Okay, so tonight the title has been on the screen for a while — I wonder if it provokes any thoughts. I am excited. This is my excited face and voice. Unless I am on the softball field.

I will try not to be too geeky, but let us talk about some application first. What do you all remember of Genesis 22? Who remembers what the chapter is about?

*[Discussion: Abraham sacrificing Isaac, trusting God, immediate obedience.]*

Good. He responded immediately — God said go now and he went. That is active obedience. And you noted that there is a question about whether Abraham trusted God to prevent the sacrifice or to resurrect Isaac after it — those are two different things, and we will consider that.

So here is the application I want you to carry through the lesson. I am connecting this passage to John 8:56 — 'Abraham rejoiced to see my day and he saw it and he was glad.' One of the biggest things I want you to take away is a better understanding and appreciation of Scripture. God is faithful to his promises from the very beginning. From Genesis, God said he was going to send someone who would make up for sin — and we see types throughout. He was faithful to the Abrahamic covenant and more.

Here is a play on words that will make more sense later: Yahweh who sees, sees to it. God sees, God knows, God provides. We will see that God the Father gave his one and only Son as a sacrifice for our sins. It is powerful to think about the fact that Yahweh provided for himself a lamb — and he himself was the lamb, the second person of the Trinity. We will see a connection: through Christ, all the nations are blessed, and in him we have our salvation and our hope of resurrection.

Open your Bibles to John 8:56, and keep a finger in Genesis 22. I will read John 8:52 through 59 for context.

## Scripture Reading: John 8:52-59

*Then the Jews said to Him, 'Now we know that You have a demon! Abraham is dead, and the prophets; and You say, If anyone keeps My word he shall never taste death. Are You greater than our father Abraham, who is dead? And the prophets are dead. Who do You make Yourself out to be?' Jesus answered, 'If I honor Myself, My honor is nothing. It is My Father who honors Me, of whom you say that He is your God. Yet you have not known Him, but I know Him. And if I say, I do not know Him, I shall be a liar like you; but I do know Him and keep His word. Your father Abraham rejoiced to see My day, and he saw it and was glad.' Then the Jews said to Him, 'You are not yet fifty years old, and have You seen Abraham?' Jesus said to them, 'Most assuredly, I say to you, before Abraham was, I AM.' Then they took up stones to throw at Him; but Jesus hid Himself and went out of the temple, going through the midst of them, and so passed by.*

There is a lot of meat in this passage. When Jesus says 'before Abraham was, I AM,' he is claiming to be Yahweh — and they took it exactly that way, and picked up stones. Now back to my question: what did Jesus mean when he said Abraham rejoiced to see his day and he saw it and was glad?

Most commentators tie this to some vision during the Abrahamic covenant appearances in Genesis 12 through 17. I found one that alluded to Genesis 22 but did not develop it. However, the Syriac church fathers from the fourth to ninth century were making this connection in their writings, so I am not on my own here. I believe it is a legitimate and compelling reading.

I prefer to say: 'Your father Abraham rejoiced that he would see my day' — which makes it more emphatic when Jesus then says, 'and he saw it and was glad.' Abraham was longing for it, and he saw it.

Now turn to Genesis 22.

## Scripture Reading: Genesis 22:1-19

*Now it came to pass after these things that God tested Abraham, and said to him, 'Abraham!' And he said, 'Here I am.' Then He said, 'Take now your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love, and go to the land of Moriah, and offer him there as a burnt offering on one of the mountains of which I shall tell you.' So Abraham rose early in the morning and saddled his donkey, and took two of his young men with him, and Isaac his son; and he split the wood for the burnt offering, and rose and went to the place of which God had told him. Then on the third day Abraham lifted his eyes and saw the place afar off. And Abraham said to his young men, 'Stay here with the donkey; the lad and I will go yonder and worship, and we will come back to you.' So Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son; and he took the fire in his hand, and a knife, and the two of them went together. But Isaac spoke to Abraham his father and said, 'My father!' And he said, 'Here I am, my son.' Then he said, 'Look, the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?' And Abraham said, 'My son, God will provide for Himself the lamb for a burnt offering.' So the two of them went together. Then they came to the place of which God had told him. And Abraham built an altar there and placed the wood in order; and he bound Isaac his son and laid him on the altar, upon the wood. And Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slay his son. But the Angel of the LORD called to him from heaven and said, 'Abraham, Abraham!' So he said, 'Here I am.' And He said, 'Do not lay your hand on the lad, or do anything to him; for now I know that you fear God, since you have not withheld your son, your only son, from Me.' Then Abraham lifted his eyes and looked, and there behind him was a ram caught in a thicket by its horns. So Abraham went and took the ram, and offered it up for a burnt offering instead of his son. And Abraham called the name of the place The-LORD-Will-Provide; as it is said to this day, 'In the Mount of the LORD it shall be provided.' Then the Angel of the LORD called to Abraham a second time out of heaven, and said: 'By Myself I have sworn, says the LORD, because you have done this thing, and have not withheld your son, your only son — blessing I will bless you, and multiplying I will multiply your descendants as the stars of the heaven and as the sand which is on the seashore; and your descendants shall possess the gate of their enemies. In your seed all the nations of the earth shall be blessed, because you have obeyed My voice.' So Abraham returned to his young men, and they rose and went together to Beersheba; and Abraham dwelt at Beersheba.*

## Verse 1: The Test

The first thing we see is that the text tells us God is testing Abraham. This is announced to us as the reader. I infer from this that when testing is announced, this is outside God's normal interaction with people — we should be prepared for something unusual. Who else in Scripture comes to mind with a unique test from God?

*[Discussion: Job, Jesus in the wilderness.]*

Job — yes. Jesus forty days in the wilderness — yes, good.

## Verse 2: Your Son, Your Only Son

Then God said: *'Take now your son, your only son Isaac, whom you love, and go to the land of Moriah.'* What is the purpose of the repetition — 'your son, your only son'? If you were Abraham and you heard that, what would that sound like?

*[Discussion: emphasis, like a punch; echoes of Jesus being the Son of God.]*

Exactly. The redundancy is for emphasis. It adds weight to the sacrifice. And you noticed the echo of the New Testament language for Jesus.

Now — how or why is Isaac the only son? Ishmael is another son. How is Isaac the only son? He is the son of promise — the one God specifically promised would come from Sarah. God said in Genesis 21:12, 'In Isaac your seed shall be called.' And then there is one more reason: Abraham had already sent Hagar and Ishmael away. So now Isaac is the son of promise, the son of Sarah, the one Abraham loves — and the one who is, in every meaningful sense, his only son.

## Verses 3-5: Three Days and a Statement of Faith

Verse 3: Abraham rose early in the morning. Immediate obedience. God said go now and he went before dawn.

On the third day, Abraham lifted his eyes and saw the place afar off. The three-day journey is significant. The Syriac church fathers read strong figurative implications here. Figuratively speaking, Isaac was dead to Abraham for three days, since God had told him to sacrifice his son. Hebrews 11:19 will confirm this reading in a moment.

Abraham said to the young men: 'Stay here with the donkey; the lad and I will go yonder and worship, and we will come back to you.' Abraham was convinced that the two of them would return. Genesis 21:12 says God told Abraham: 'In Isaac your seed shall be called.' If Isaac is the seed, Isaac has to survive. So however Abraham processed the command to sacrifice Isaac, he knew somehow they were both coming back.

Hebrews 11:17-19 makes it explicit: *By faith Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac, and he who had received the promises offered up his only begotten son, of whom it was said, 'In Isaac your seed shall be called,' concluding that God was able to raise him up, even from the dead, from which he also received him in a figurative sense.* The implication from Hebrews is that Abraham expected to have to kill Isaac, burn him on the altar, and that God would raise him from the dead — all in time for them to return to the young men together.

## Verses 6-8: The Wood and the Question

Verse 6: Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son. The lumber, the timber of the cross, was laid across the shoulders of the sacrifice. Isaac carrying his own sacrificial wood — just as Christ carried the cross. The fire and the knife remained in the father's hands. Even though Christ gave himself willingly, it was the Father who wielded the fire and knife, so to speak.

Verse 7: Isaac spoke and said, 'My father!' He said, 'Here I am, my son.' Then Isaac asked: 'Look, the fire and the wood, but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?'

Verse 8: Abraham said, 'My son, God will provide for Himself the lamb for a burnt offering.' Notice: God is placed first in the Hebrew clause. Moses is stressing that God is the key figure. God will provide for himself the lamb.

Now, was Abraham being prophetic when he said this? I think it is in two senses. He may have been uncertain about what would happen — he was doing everything necessary to kill Isaac, which is why Hebrews says he believed God would raise him from the dead. So 'God will provide' at that moment was Abraham's honest faith: God will sort this out somehow. Then after the ram appeared and he named the place Yahweh Yireh, it became prophetic — 'in the mount of the LORD it shall be provided' points forward to Christ on this same mountain.

## Verses 9-12: The Altar and the Angel

The narrative slows in verse 9 as each step is captured: Abraham built an altar, placed the wood in order, bound Isaac, laid him on the altar on the wood. Then verse 10: Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slay his son.

What do you picture when you hear 'Abraham stretched out his hand'? Most people picture Abraham raising the knife above his head. But there is an alternative reading from Leviticus 1:4: *Then he shall put his hand on the head of the burnt offering, and it will be accepted on his behalf to make atonement for him.* The ritual act for a burnt offering involved placing one's hand on the head of the sacrifice. So it is possible that when Abraham stretched out his hand, he was placing it on the head of his son Isaac — on the head of the son that he loves — before he takes the knife. I stumbled across artwork depicting this scene in various ways, and that image is a powerful one.

Then the Angel of the LORD called from heaven: 'Abraham, Abraham!' He said, 'Here I am.' And the angel said: 'Do not lay your hand on the lad, or do anything to him; for now I know that you fear God, since you have not withheld your son, your only son, from Me.'

A note on the Hebrew: the word for angel is *malach* and the word for knife is *ma'achelet* — they are very similar sounding words. The angel of the LORD stopped the knife. The Angel of the LORD is the pre-incarnate Christ — the second person of the Trinity.

The phrase 'now I know' marks the conclusion of the test from verse 1. Some Syriac texts and Jewish writings translate this as 'now you have made known that you fear God' or 'I have made known' — the implication being that God already knew what Abraham would do, having foreordained it before the foundation of the world, but that here it is being demonstrated publicly. Both readings are legitimate from the Syriac.

## Coming Next Week

We have reached verse 12 and it is 8:01 — so we will stop here. Come back next week. I am going to tell you what the word *provide* really means. We will look at themes running through the passage. We will tie more on the connection between Isaac and Jesus. And we will look at what Mount Moriah is and why it is relevant to where Jesus went to the cross.

So come back. I am excited. Let us pray.

Heavenly Father, we do ask that you bless our studies. I pray that it would be an encouragement to your people. I am grateful for this opportunity to study your Word, and I pray that you would help me with my words as we continue next week. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.
